text:
A lot of people now use AI assistants to write code. Cursor, Claude Code, Copilot. You can change dozens of files in a day. You committed, the code runs. But do you really know what changed?

This is the knowledge gap of vibe coding. AI implements the feature, but the changes never become your own knowledge. Next time you face a similar problem, you still have to ask AI again.

Ahadiff exists to solve this. Ahadiff does not just generate a commit summary. Its core idea is called verified diff learning, with three layers. First, it only explains this one change, not the whole repository. Second, every sentence in the lesson must point to a specific file and line number. This is the claim-evidence chain. Third, a deterministic verifier checks whether each conclusion matches the code. What you learn is traceable and verifiable, not a model hallucination.

Ahadiff extracts every knowledge claim from the lesson, then checks each one. Each claim gets one of five statuses: Verified means it matches the code. Weak means partial match. Not Proven means no evidence found. Contradicted means it conflicts with code. And Rejected means completely wrong.

In our real learning records, you can see each claim annotated with its source file and line number. Click the dot on the diff page, and the right panel shows that code snippet. This is not a demo. It is the result of 161 real learning runs.

The workflow is straightforward. After AI changes your code, run one `learn` command, specifying the commit range. Ahadiff goes through an eight-layer pipeline: capture diff, assemble context, redact secrets, generate three-tier lessons, extract and verify claims, create quiz questions, score the result, and write to the review database. Then open the web UI. The lesson page shows the full lesson, and the diff page shows line-by-line annotations. Three lesson tiers — full, hint, and compact — suit different learning depths.

Just reading is passive learning. You will not remember it. Ahadiff acquires two core methods from cognitive science. First is active recall. The quiz page shows multiple-choice questions where you must think of the answer yourself, instead of passively reading. Second is spaced repetition. The review page schedules review cards using the FSRS-6 algorithm. FSRS is currently the most advanced open-source spaced repetition algorithm, more accurate than Anki's default SM-2. The system currently holds 87 active review cards, each bound to specific knowledge points and code sources.

How do you know the learning result is reliable? Ahadiff scores on eight dimensions: accuracy, evidence, diff coverage, learnability, quiz transfer, spec alignment, conciseness, and safety-privacy. Maximum score is 100. Pass threshold is 80. Accuracy and evidence have hard gates. Below the threshold means automatic failure.

In our latest real run, the eight-dimension total was 76.17, with quiz transfer scoring a perfect 10, and safety-privacy also 10. These scores are deterministically computed, not model self-assessment.

Ahadiff does not discard what you learned. Each learning session extracts new concepts, written to `concepts.gql`, deduplicated by term key. The concepts page shows a knowledge graph, where nodes are concepts and edges are relationships. You can see which concepts you have mastered and which are still weak. This design draws from Karpathy's LLM Wiki idea and Graphify's code structure graph, but lands as a diff-level learning overlay that adds a learning layer on top of the code map.

Ahadiff's design draws from five projects. Taravel's AutoSearch inspired the `n-file` contract and diff ratchet mechanism. ZJU RLL's Skill0 inspired section-level helpfulness feedback. Darwin-Skill inspired the eight-dimension evaluation and phase 2.5 structural rewrite. Graphify inspired the seven-state freshness state machine. Karpathy's LLM Wiki inspired the concept accumulation design. But the actual implementation has gone beyond the original blueprint. All eight architectural layers are implemented, 161 real runs completed, 2,530 backend unit tests passing, 365 frontend tests passing, and 2,045 end-to-end tests passing.

To summarize, Ahadiff solves the knowledge internalization problem after AI coding. It uses verified diff learning to ensure what you learn is real, evidence-backed knowledge, not summaries or hallucinations. Try it. Install, initialize, configure your model, then `learn` a recent change. Open the web UI to read lessons, check evidence, take quizzes, and review periodically. Let AI write code. Let Ahadiff return the knowledge to you.
